summary: Vic Hall was my friend who helped me during the physical exam by copying the eye charts for me. I remember relying on him because my left eye was 20/30, and I needed to pass the vision test. He was there early on in my training journey, supporting me when I needed it most.

I ended up helping win World War II by becoming a pilot, even though I had to con my way into the pilot classification. The war shaped my entire training and combat experience, from primary training to flying 67 combat missions in the Pacific theater. It was a time of intense pressure, danger, and determination.

Many of the guys I trained with had been in the Civilian Pilot Training Program before the war and could solo in just a few hours. I was the only one who hadn’t flown before, which made my training much harder. Their experience set a high bar that I had to work extra hard to reach.

Advanced training took place in Stockton, California, where I received my wings and was commissioned as a second lieutenant. It was a significant milestone because it marked the transition from basic training to becoming a full-fledged pilot. Stockton was where I felt I truly earned my place as an aviator.

After Stockton, I was stationed at Mather Field in Sacramento, California. I came home quite often from there, which was a comfort during the intense training period. Mather Field was where we studied B-25s and prepared for the next phase of flying.

Sacramento was home base for me when I was at Mather Field. It was close enough that I could come home frequently, which helped keep my spirits up. The city was a backdrop to some of the most important training stages in my career.

We trained extensively on the B-25 Mitchell, studying its hydraulic, electrical, and fuel systems. Later, I flew a brand new B-25 D-model in combat with the 17th Bombardment Squadron. The B-25 was central to many of my missions across the Pacific, including dangerous low-altitude strafing runs.

I trained on A-20 Havocs in Oklahoma City and Greenville, South Carolina, before heading to New Guinea. One memorable moment was when I flew an A-20 at nearly 400 miles per hour, pushing the limits of the plane. The A-20 was a tough, fast aircraft that I learned to handle under challenging conditions.

Oklahoma City was where I trained on the A-20 Havoc after primary and basic training. It was part of the progression that prepared me for combat flying. The training there was intense and focused on mastering the aircraft’s capabilities.

After Oklahoma City, I went to Greenville, South Carolina, to continue flying B-25s. It was the last stop before heading overseas to New Guinea. Greenville was where I polished my skills and got ready for the real combat missions ahead.

We flew in a C-47 Skytrain all the way to New Guinea after completing our stateside training. The C-47 was our transport to the combat zone, marking the end of training and the beginning of real action. It was a long flight that brought us face-to-face with the realities of war.

New Guinea was where we were stationed at Nadzab, thirty miles inland from Lae. Conditions were tough—no fresh food, just canned goods and coffee—and we dealt with supply issues, including a local Australian who controlled the good stuff. It was a harsh environment but the launching point for many combat missions.

Nadzab was our base in New Guinea, located inland from Lae. It was a rough place with limited supplies, and we had to get creative to get better food and gear. I remember meeting an Australian named Mitchell there who controlled the unloading of Liberty ships and traded goods with us.

Lae was the coastal town near our base at Nadzab in New Guinea. It was where the Australian Mitchell lived and managed the unloading of Liberty ships, though he kept most of the good stuff for himself. Lae was an important logistical point in the Pacific theater.

Liberty ships were the cargo vessels that brought supplies to New Guinea, but the good food and equipment often didn’t reach us because of theft. An Australian named Mitchell controlled the unloading at Lae and kept the best items for himself. We negotiated with him to get some supplies in exchange for parachute silk and trench knives.

Salamaua was a source of fresh fruit that Mitchell could get for us if we traded him parachute silk. It was a valuable commodity since we mostly had canned food at Nadzab. Getting fruit from Salamaua was a small but important morale boost for us in the jungle.

We made a trip down to Australia to ferry back B-25s, but there were none available, so I took an A-20 instead. Australia was a brief but memorable stop where I pushed the A-20 to its limits in a high-speed flight. It was a rare moment of excitement before returning to the combat zone.

I was assigned to the 17th Bombardment Squadron after being grounded for two weeks. It was called the 'suicide squadron' because we flew low-altitude strafing and bombing missions that were extremely dangerous. Despite the risks, it turned out to be the best assignment since many others in medium altitude units were later shot down.

The 17th Squadron was part of the 71st Bombardment Group, and together we flew brand new B-25 D-models into combat. We conducted missions over Mindoro, Luzon, Formosa, and other targets, focusing on alcohol plants, munitions factories, and airfields. The group was a tight-knit unit facing constant danger.

Mindoro was one of the key locations where we flew combat missions, often under direct theater orders from General MacArthur. We targeted Japanese rolling stock and infrastructure there, flying low and fast to avoid detection. The missions were intense and critical to the campaign in the Philippines.

Luzon was a major target during our combat operations, especially the northern areas near Clark Field. We attacked trains, trucks, and other military targets to disrupt Japanese movements. Luzon was a dangerous place, and one mission there resulted in my plane being heavily damaged by ground fire.

Formosa was among the many places we flew missions over, targeting strategic enemy facilities. It was part of the broader campaign to weaken Japanese forces in the Pacific. Flying over Formosa was risky, but necessary to achieve our objectives.

Clark Air Base on Luzon was a significant Japanese airfield that we targeted during our missions. The Japanese were moving from Clark Field northward, and our orders included wiping out their rolling stock in the area. Clark Air Base was a focal point in the fight for control of the Philippines.

Bataan was a rendezvous point during one of our missions where we were supposed to regroup after attacking Japanese targets. Unfortunately, no one showed up there to meet us, so we had to turn back alone. Bataan was a key location in the Philippines campaign and held a lot of strategic importance.

I earned the Distinguished Flying Cross for two missions where I knocked out a lot of enemy trains and trucks, cleaning out roads and highways under heavy fire. One mission in particular was over Luzon, where my plane was badly damaged but I managed to bring everyone home safely. Receiving this award was a proud moment that recognized the risks and skill involved in those flights.

Harry Hall was my radio gunner during combat missions, and he had a close call when our plane was hit over Luzon. Gasoline sprayed all over him, putting out his cigarette and knocking him off his stool, but thankfully he wasn’t injured. Harry was a vital part of the crew, and moments like that showed how dangerous our missions really were.

General Douglas MacArthur was the commanding officer who issued direct theater orders to our squadron. One mission I flew on Mindoro came straight from him, with strict instructions not to strafe villages. MacArthur’s leadership was a constant presence in our operations in the Philippines.

Manila Bay was part of the area around Manila where we flew missions, although it wasn’t mentioned in detail in my story. It was a strategic location in the Philippines campaign. The bay and surrounding areas were heavily contested during the liberation efforts.

Corregidor was a significant site in the Philippines, known for its defense during the early war years. While I didn’t describe specific missions there, it was part of the broader theater of operations where we flew combat sorties. Corregidor symbolized the fierce fighting in the Pacific.

Cavite was another location in the Philippines campaign, though I didn’t mention it specifically in my combat stories. It was part of the region where we operated and targeted Japanese forces. Cavite was important for naval and air operations in the area.

Manila was the capital city near many of the missions we flew in the Philippines. It was a key target and a place of intense fighting as we worked to liberate the islands. The city and its surroundings were central to the campaign I was part of.
